title: Talking with Jared Isaacman about NASA and the future and jets.
description:
I had a chance to catch up with Jared Isaacman about his run for NASA administrator and his future! I'm always excited to learn more about what Jared's got going on.

--------------------------

Want to support what I do? Consider becoming a Patreon supporter for access to exclusive livestreams, our discord channel! - http://patreon.com/everydayastronaut

Or become a YouTube member for some bonus perks as well! - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6uKrU_WqJ1R2HMTY3LIx5Q/join

All music is original! Find it anywhere you listen to music! (Spotify, iTunes, Google Play, Amazon, etc)
full_text: Jared, good to see you. Long time. Last time I spoke to you, you were in orbit and you had just done the first private spacew walk and uh it's really good to see you again. How are you doing? Yeah. Yeah, it's good to see you again, too. And uh so an awful lot has happened since then. It feels like forever ago. An awful lot has happened and it feels like a lot has has changed and a lot of uh your plans have have kind of changed and I'm really excited to catch up with you on kind of what's next for you. Um I don't really want to linger too much on like you know what had happened in the last 6 months for you but one big question because I didn't see this coming at all in the first place is had you thought about being NASA administrator like was that ever a plan in your head is it ever something you had thought or considered what was that like just tell me that were you wanting to do this in the first place or what was that like no I mean I had no idea I mean I wasn't you know a politically active person I wasn't active in the campaign. I mean, also like we were getting ready to go to space and we were in quarantine and such, so it wasn't exact time for it. Um, yeah, relatively like uh apolitical. I will say though that I've always had an interest in um in politics and um you know I actually always assumed it would be much more of an appointed position than it would be a uh you know running for an elected office cuz I I'm I'm generally that kind of person like sure I bring people together and I feel like in any election you know if you make your way if you wind up with 53% of people tolerating you you did okay that's not generally my speed um but uh I never would have expected this to come uh but I was thrilled to have an opportunity to do it. It was uh it was a thrilling journey and I know as crazy as it sounds uh and I've said it a few times even knowing the outcome like I would do it again. It was um it was something that you'd uh yeah just unlike anything else out there it's learning a new language and um to me the best part is is once you learn enough to know that you actually can make a difference um and overcome that intimidation factor a little bit it gets even more exciting because you yeah you believe you can help. Yeah. And I on that note, I got to say, mad props again, just a huge amount of respect. The way you've handled I mean, just the way you communicate online in general has always been uh I I think really encouraging and something to look up to. But seeing how you've handled everything the last month has been another exemplary example of of of what I want all of the internet to be doing and especially anyone public figure in politics and stuff like that. you just handled it all so well and always taking the high road, always uh having a lot of respect for people that you're speaking with, even if you disagree and I huge amount of respect for that. Oh, well, yeah, I appreciate that. It was um I was thrilled to do it. Don't really fault anyone. I mean, you go into you go into politics like you can be a piece on the board and not even know it. Uh so, I think I assumed at some point or another I was going to get fired. Um so, it just happened a little bit earlier than expected, but uh again, happy to have a shot. this point. I'm just uh hoping we get a new nominee in there pretty quick. It's uh it's it's got to be very hard on Janet as the acting administrator. Um you know, you need the you need the political juice to get things done and that's going to require, you know, someone going through the confirmation process. And there's a lot for NASA to be doing and a lot of programs that need saving, a lot of changes that still need to be made, a lot of uh important things. What were you I guess not even even from the inside or outside. What's the one thing you really want to see NASA do in the next 5 years? What's like the big the big thing that you think is like if NASA doesn't do this in the next 5 years, we're in deep trouble? Well, I'd hit on two things and I um you know, it's uh it's not that I don't um it's not that I mind at all talking about like the what would have you know, what what it could have been or what I would have done or something like that. It's just that I think it's unfair kind of to to Janet and others who are in there that are I mean treading water in a turbulent environment um to go too deep in it. But what what I what I would say is one like the agency does need a reorganization but that is not unique to NASA. I mean that is governmentwide. We move way too slow as a nation. um like we we we're we're in this period of stagnation where a lot of our skills of doing big bold things um have atrophied while you have rivals out there that are like hitting their stride and just moving fast and that's a dangerous spot to be in um and and NASA you know is it it fits within that. So I don't think we're going to be able to do the extraordinary things uh very quickly without some reorganization. Like you do have to get rid of a lot of that um that bureaucracy that you know excessive amount of management and decision-m layers that um just impede progress. Um and alongside it is a lot of little pet projects like thousands of them tens of thousands of them. And they I mean you know you you think about like say at SpaceX where just as soon as they figured out Falcon 1 Elon's like delete it we're moving on to Falcon 9 probably could have made a great business keeping both around for some period of time but he knew one would have been a distraction and come at an opportunity cost for the bigger prize. Um there's like a million examples of that inside NASA where like a lot of little programs that sure in isolation are like well that's a nice thing to do that's a nice thing to do but if you have enough of them it take it prevents you from doing like the big near impossible things that every taxpayer is happy to contribute into NASA to doing so that to me is like one broad reorganization and then if there was like one other thing I would have loved to do and was trying to plant seeds on it is you know uh is to give the senators in the SLS states the next thing to to move on to and to me that was a that would have been a scaled up multi-billion dollar nuclear electric propulsion program um because they're all receptive to it uh you meet with all these senators you know sometimes multiple times and sure there's a lot of jobs there there's a lot of spending but I mean they've evolved I mean Mashe went from making landing crafts in World War II to Saturn 5 to shuttle to SLS they're happy to move on to the next great thing but If you don't give them that, then they're certainly going to get entrenched and dig into what they have and uh and moving on after, you know, we we get the the lunar objectives done into something that we're going to need if we really want to, you know, if we really want to explore, especially deeper in the solar system where, you know, solar power is going to be, you know, less of a factor as you get farther away. Um, not to mention it takes a lot of pressure off all the institute resource manufacturing, which is still a lot of miracles that need to happen on the surface in order to come back home. So when you say nuclear electric propulsion, I've heard you talk I I I think you know I read you mentioned that with uh with Eric Burgerer at ours and um are you talking about using like an RTG and then like a like an ion thruster type of layout or are you talking about uh any interest in like you know regular old radio or thermonuclear uh you know like like the the Nerva engine and things like that like where does your interest lie? Well, I mean, look, um I I too love that like Nervo goes back like, you know, probably 60 years or or so at this point in time, but there's a lot of drawbacks in my mind with nuclear thermal. For starters, like open air testing is going to be very problematic cuz it's going to spray radioactive juice out there. Um second, you know, you you're still going to have to top it off with hydrogen. Um, unless you're making these like as disposable vehicles, in which case you're better off just sending a Starship on a one-way mission, then like to me the real advantage is being able to move a lot of mass efficiently from point A to B. Literally, I mean, the to me the the analogy is the the transcontinental railroad. Even today, you got to move a lot of mass across the country. Railroad has some efficiency advantages. Well, if we can have nuclear tugs, although I just think tug doesn't sound as cool as like a nuclear spaceship that can be going back and forth to lrangee points to to Mars to be on. I mean, if there's something interesting going on with the Europa Clipper mission and we have this, you know, thirst for knowledge, we're going to want to send things that can go back and forth. There's not going to be any good, you know, insitue resource manufacturing there anytime soon. So, nuclear electric has a lot of advantages. Um it gets nuclear reactors in space like highlyenriched ian uranium reactors that we can use for uh surface power if required. And then just terms of technical hurdles marrying an a nuclear reactor and just you're just changing it you know uh from like solar power to an ion thruster to um to nuclear power like you you have minimal technical risks there. it um it's just scaling up a lot of technology that you already know will work and um and that I just see as advantageous because again back to nuclear thermal you have testing issues and again you're not going to be able to have this train going back and forth you're going to have to top it off with hydrogen um and I just think that's comes with its own set of challenges 100% I I gotta admit this is one of the first times I've really thought about like a nuclear electric like tug or system because I it's not the first thing that comes to mind And when I when I hear nuclear, I think, you know, yeah, I'm thinking I'm thinking Nerva. I'm thinking the old um that kind of stuff. And and to me, it has been a shame that, you know, we had those completely tested and ready to fly. And then both us and the Soviets, the Soviets had the uh I don't remember RD 901 or something, 801, one of them, uh was was a nuclear almost exact same thing, even higher efficiency than the Nerva, smaller so it could fly on proton. And it's just a shame that like this technologies like that. I'm like, I wish we had just flown that, you know, just because the potential there is is really so great. It's it's just so great. We got to start somewhere, right? I mean, and then we can build upon it. It's just like you look at the evolution of a lot of chemical propulsion today. Even take like the Merlin, like the performance you get out of it today versus its initial variation. So, like once we get comfortable with nuclear reactors in space, nuclear electric, we will undoubtedly improve the performance over time. But also, it's all we we just simply don't have a choice. There's no good way you're coming home from Mars uh without getting a nuclear reactor to that surface. Like there's no way you're going to be able to do your, you know, propellant manufacturing there without that power source. So, we're we're committed to this path. Why not make it why not use it to help us get there? And then there are DoD applications to it, too. I mean, um you know, that could make a lot of sense in, you know, the context of like a Golden Dome or something like that. So, there there's just so many good reasons why we should go in that direction. and get it out of the lab where it's, you know, you know, subkillow power type things and get into the real megawatt, you know, USS Nautilus type program, you know. So, now that you've I mean, you've already had a really good experience with the public and government side of things, especially with with Draen, you you have probably a unique, you know, experience that very few people actually have that relationship. Um what in your mind is like the biggest hurdle then as far as actually doing something like this as far as getting megawatt uh you know class reactors in space? What what does it actually take to to do that? Does it require like NASA government support? Could the private sector start doing it on their own without where are we at with that kind of thing? Well well that was the nice part about it being a a major NASA program is that I I mean I do think it needs to be a government initiative for now. So I mean that's the hard part like there were so many questions as I was going through this confirmation process of like even why do we even need NASA you know now that we have great you know commercial space companies like SpaceX and and Jeff Bezos funding Blue Origin. It was like what are we talking about here? Of course we still need NASA and like you don't think back in the 1960s that we didn't have dependencies on Grumman and other major aerospace manufacturers. Of course we do. The issue is just we need to recalibrate from time to time of what we're working on versus what they're working on. And right now you're at this interesting point in time where we're working on big heavy lift rockets while commercial industry is capable of doing it. Why are we doing that? Like we should recalibrate to what they can't work on that doesn't have a business case or or any good commercial use case. And that's why like nuclear electric propulsion was so important. And and the and the reason why um it should be a government program is because you're talking about like highlyenriched uranium here um and launching nuclear reactors which for for corporations to get you know like indemnity waiverss to it is going to be a very long and painful process. Even transporting highlyenriched uranium is a is a nightmare. It's like the exact kind of thing that we are paying as taxpayers the government to solve because the applications for it are are numerous and it's just again in the direction of goodness. So, um, I think it it it does need to be a government program and and and for all the cool terrestrial micronuclear reactor companies that are trying to solve real problems here, which I'm totally bullish on, like we with AI and everything else, we need more power and um, and you've got some smart people working on it, but they're all going into like a direction of really lowenriched, super safe fuels, which makes sense, but when we're in space, um, there's a lot of radiation out there anyway. Um, and if we want to be efficient on mass, we're going to want to run really high temperatures and we're not going to want to refuel those reactors ever. So, what you need is is highly enriched uranium to do it, of which it's super controlled, hard to manage. Again, it's just all it's all the reasons why this should be the evolution of of SLS um as a government program. It's it is very refreshing to hear something like this where where it's an exciting technology that is completely like within the realm of of what we can do right now. Absolutely. And to say like it would be really cool if someone was actually actively working on that. I think that's where a lot of the frustration with uh you know with SLS comes from from a lot of people is it's not anything new or exciting and and in a lot of ways it's almost you know it's like worse performing than the Saturn 5. you know, that that is meant to replace and it's like that's not very exciting. You know, that's just objectively not that exciting. Um whereas, you know, a new technology, anything like that or new science mission, you know, Dragonfly type mission and obviously, you know, Curiosity and all the, you know, all the things before those are exciting, you know, those are those are exciting and novel and unique and and only that's what NASA does best. And it's it is kind of frustrating to watch as you know SLS is sort of just flown once and so far we're waiting for it to fly again and it's uh costing a lot of money every year that it's not flying anyway. It's like man this there's got to be a better way to do this kind of stuff. Yeah. to I mean it's um I I wish we kind of I love work like kind of working backwards from a um like a desired outcome when approaching projects and I wish we um I wish that was applied a little bit more towards uh towards Artemis. Now look at the time I don't fault anyone for how we wound up in this situation. I mean, this was conceived, you know, you didn't have self-landing rockets like and if you're going to if you're going to, you know, try and get a lot of support for a multi multi-billion dollar space program, you're going to want to you're going to want to repurpose a lot of hardware that gives you the the commercial lobby, the the the political lobby within the states. So, I don't blame us for how we got into it, but I mean, you know, at some point in time or another, you know, there are there is some good money going after bad and um you know, the mobile launcher 2 uh debacle is a is a travesty. You know, everybody, you know, it's people are, you know, screaming about the the science budget and at the same time, you know, we can't compromise on SLS or Artemis. is like, you know, how much we could have funded if we didn't have to be in the the mobile launcher 2 business or how much quicker we probably could have gotten to the moon if uh you know, if JAXA, probably one of the most capable international space agencies, hands down, was contributing something to the actual get to the moon part instead of a six or seven billion dollar pressurized rover for the moon. um you know like they could have applied those resources into something that helps us get there and then we can figure out what our if we if we actually necessitate such an extraordinary amount of resources into a pressurized rover. I I don't know again it's hard you're playing hindsight on on a lot of these things. It was that to me was much more of a not assign assigning blame as it relates to SLS. Let's use what we got to get the job done and then pivot in the right direction um and start working on what's next, you know. So you you I mean you were talking pretty openly about really genuinely getting humans to Mars and I mean where do you actually in terms of feasibility you know like you mentioned I mean we're really unless we're going to I think it's something like 10 acres of solar you know unless we have a nuclear reactor on Mars we don't have enough energy to to produce you know the fuel to get home where are you on the scale of like this is actually going to be happening you know in the next 5 10 years like What's your gut feeling? What what's your hope? What's your what what and what do you most importantly I guess what's your biggest the biggest hurdle do you think? Well, I mean I I was approaching it I think from uh from two perspectives. One which is already in works which is we can't land on the moon unless one of two providers commercial providers has kind of perfected their heavy lift reusable technology. So if we have that then we have the means at least to accelerate mass to the velocities necessary to either get to the moon or Mars. That's a pretty damn important first step. So simply by when when I people were like you can't afford to do both moon and Mars. It's like well we are parallel tracking some pretty important technology right from the get-go. If we can get to the moon and actually have American astronauts set foot on the moon then we've got a pretty important capability. So now to me it was how do you take the like what problems can you solve that industry can't in order to better enable um a a Mars uh endeavor? And to me that's where nuclear electric propulsion came in and that's where I would have tried to pivot so many resources because look there is potentially a world where if you did scale up to megawatt class nuclear electric propulsion and I think you know America being underway under nuclear power you know in the next four years is very real possibility then you could potentially get humans to the surface of Mars and back but not necessarily in you know how we're currently thinking about doing it which is like a complete end type Starship approach But you could take a lot of the burden off of Starship uh and use NEP to get there and it might be a a longer mission than what's currently contemplated. Uh but you wouldn't require insitue resource manufacturing which to me is like I mean if you think about how many resources are at Starbase, you know, working on stage zero under 1G under an atmosphere and it still doesn't always go right. Imagine that on on the on on the surface of Mars. um I'd rather bet on an alternative means to help in that transportation process. And again, it's whether it's Mars or not, it's it's still in the right direction that government spending should be going for um you know, for space projects. And not to mention going right as far as a launch, just even imagining all the you know, dozens of trucks that line up down the road to refuel. I mean, obviously you're refueling super heavy, too, but just that visual of like how much propellant it actually takes to move a vehicle of this size. And now imagine trying to do that on Mars with nothing with absolutely nothing there. And you have to bring it all with you and and I mean it is a monumental task. Like that's that's a lot harder I think than most people realize when they imagine uh a Mars return trip is how hard it's going to be to actually refuel on the surface. And yeah, I mean it's you're right though about you know there's a lot of parallel tracks there. the and getting off Earth and getting a lot of mass into orbit is is one of the hardest things to solve at scale and and to make it inexpensive. And that is one of those things where, you know, you said it you said it already where we're already relying on, you know, on Starship and Super Heavy and Blue Origin to land on the moon in the first place. And I think that's almost why it's so why SLS is feeling even more and more like, you know, well, if we already are needing these other vehicles to get on the moon, what what's the point of this thing, you know? And I don't know. It's been a weird uh mental gymnastics for me for the past few years to to go from SLS is is necessary to SLS is I don't know it's it's still necessary to me because we still don't really have another immediate viable option and but it'll very quickly become obsolete you know as soon as Yeah. Yeah. I mean, look, it's just you pick the mission, right? And uh to me, it was like we should be ready to have those conversations after Artemis 3 after and and look, I think even again I said if you terminate for convenience now, you probably even have enough hardware for an Artemis 4, you got to pay every vendor that's basically as soon as they turn started turning a wrench on something, you have to see it through uh to essentially its completion. So, you have enough SLS's to like, you know, have one or two shots on goal for a landing. And at that point, you need some off-ramp. It's just unaffordable. And no one should be excited about um you know uh seeing astronauts on the moon every 2 or 3 years. Like that's not winning. Um that's totally terrible. And look, Gateway um you know, there's no logical reason uh for that. Like you're not going to learn anything by keeping astronauts uh trapped there. It's not an ideal environment for them. Either keep them in lower Earth orbit where they have more protection or send them to Mars where you have an atmosphere. But um if anything I was like maybe we could repurpose you know gateway modules and put them on an NEP uh reactor and use that to to get people going in the right direction. Now we're talking. Yeah. I mean at some point or another there needs to be an offramp for it. The thing is you're hearing it from others like uh Scott Pace I think he testified in front of uh you know the Congressional Space Subcommittee three months ago you know very big SLS supporter early on uh with the National Space Council and he said look an off-ramp is inevitable at this point like we are going to have to move on to commercial if we want any you know real frequency for lunar operations. Uh, I could talk SLS and and the and the future of the Artemis program and Mars all day, but uh, I think I want to kind of I don't want to spend too much of your time talking about that stuff because I'm mostly curious too about what is next for you now. I mean, are you going to be going back into the the players program? You know, we still have two more missions, you know, that you had to put on on on hiatus here. Uh, what's your current feeling on that stuff and and just life in general? Yeah, it's a good question. I'm kind of I mentioned it earlier when we were chatting, but uh you know, first time in 26 years since I was 16, I didn't have some sort of a, you know, uh professional obligation or any major endeavor underway. So, I'm enjoying the summer flying, skydiving, spending time with family, but I'm also information gathering. I I uh I uh I said I I don't think I've flown my last mission, but it could take different forms. So, I wouldn't be shocked if there's uh you know, additional commercial space missions on the horizon. I also wouldn't be shocked if maybe I do stick around in politics a little bit. Um it's uh like I do like helping and trying to bring people together and I love a good challenge and that's really hard to do in politics is to bring people together and and and try and make things better. And um I don't know once I kind of crossed that point where I actually felt like the like I said the intimidation factor of being in that arena uh you know had subsided that I thought I could make a difference. It's uh it's kind of appealing. But um I will let you know like one thing that I am committed to. I was uh I um I'm buying these uh these uh F5s. Um so for those that saw the original Top Gun, that was the MIG 28 was the uh was the F5. And um it's basically a much more it's a juicier version of a T38. Like way juicier. Um so it looks like a NASA T38 from a distance, but um far greater performance. Is that single engine? I forget. Is that one of the big differences? Still still too. Okay. No, it's just upgraded. It's like uh probably at least 33% more thrust, but it also has uh like maneuvering flaps, so it's much more maneuverable. And um it's what they use at Top Gun for aggressors. It's um and they still do. Anyway, um so I was buying these uh when I thought I was going into the NASA job and uh I was going to paint them in like a contemporary NASA colors. um mostly take the stress off the T38 fleet at NASA which are pretty banged up and use them for NASA's inspirational purpose like rocket launches, air shows, kind of the things I do already right now except you know it's not very patriotic to fly a MIG 29 over a rocket launch get back to American jets. So anyway, I'm still going through with that for sure. And um so I'll put like a little team together to do uh you know to go to space related air shows and uh big rocket launches using these F5s and hopefully like a cool like space theme color. So that's like a very small side project that I'm enjoying. Um but I'm sure there'll be more. So remind me the T. What's the the fighter jet where the the uh horizontal stabils are like is that the F4 where they're kind of like downward pointing? Yeah, that's okay. Yeah. And then the F5 I'm trying in uh the California Science Museum there's there's a T38 in the in the entryway and there's another version of it that's a single engine T38 but I don't remember what that is and I that could be the F20 that was um it was uh it's actually Chuck Joerger was like the spokesperson for him. They took the F5 and then they put a a single I don't know Pratt Whitney probably early F-16 motor on it and it was a a high performer. I think they only made two or three and then they were trying to market them to compete with the F-16 overseas and then it w up going to the F-16. But the F5, if you saw at the movie Top Gun, the black jet with the red star, the M28, that's an F5. That's funny. It does look just like a T38, but it's way more maneuverable, way juicier. Um, anyway, does it have afterburners? Of course. Of course it does. Oh, so it can fly. So it's supersonic then, too. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Okay, that's awesome. So, so you're not willing to say you're not like I I just I got to imagine a world where we get you back in a dragon here in the next few years cuz I mean those the things you've already accomplished, you know, were just some of the coolest missions ever. And especially uh Players Dawn was incredible and and the idea of a you know, a Hubble rescue mission wherever that may lay these days is still about the most exciting thing I can possibly even imagine. Um, but I I'm I'm silently rooting that you that you're able to not silently, I'll say it out loud. I'm still rooting that you get back up in there and do some more cool uh you know, science and missions like that. I hope so. I'm I'm I'm sure there'll be uh something and somebody's going to have to solve that Hubble sooner than later. Um it's uh its orbit is decaying pretty rapidly. Um it's going to be a real problem in the next couple years. Um which is kind of a bummer. Um, I can't imagine they're gonna there's a world where they're going to let it um have an uncontrolled re-entry. So hopefully they do something there um on that on that one. I would have definitely had it been confirmed, I sure as hell would have released probably the uh the uh feasibility study on that one. Uh so that at least tells people how what the recommendation really was. But um I don't know. We'll see what uh we'll see what comes up. Yeah. anything else you you think of uh that that's an important thing to to let everyone know that that is new with you or something you're excited to work on as a sign off? No, I mean I think look no matter what I'm going to I've been uh kind of a champion for uh NASA and commercial space for for a long time. Um I think that will I will certainly continue to do so. I would definitely um I know it's been a turbulent time and uh you know people are very down especially when they look at you know some of the proposed budget cuts but let me let me tell you from you have to meet with every pretty much every senator uh as part of the confirmation process and whether they're Republican or Democrat they love NASA. They love NASA and um and they're both were willing to fight for it. I mean you saw that on full display during my my hearing. So, look, um, we definitely have to do some things to get, uh, you know, to get get the to close the gap on the on the deficit and get closer to a balance balanced budget. Um, but I do think like NASA is going to be okay. It's uh, it's a it will still be a very big budget. You still have two of the world's wealthiest people, you know, pouring their own resources into um, you know, enabling human spaceflight capabilities and exploring among the stars. It's it's going to be okay. like we are much we we in terms of like space's darkest days versus its greatest days, we are way closer to the greatest days are are ahead. Um and we'll get through the turbulent period. I really believe it. So, uh that's refreshing to hear and I really always have admired your uh your perspective and like I said, you have you have such a unique perspective on so many things. So, it's always a pleasure to talk to you and hopefully next time I see you, it's again uh maybe doing some of the fighter jets or uh or maybe flying on another mission at some point. Hey, that sounds great. Uh I'll let you know when those uh those F5s start showing up if you want to come out. Oh, that's awesome. I would I would greatly appreciate it. I will be there. Awesome. Well, thank you so much for your time, Jared. Good hearing from you. Yeah, good catching up, Tim. Take care, man. Thank you.
